summary: I remember where I was on Pearl Harbor Day very clearly. It was December 7, 1941, and I was in North Zulch visiting family for the weekend. My brother and I only found out about the attack when we returned to Conroe that Sunday afternoon around 4 PM from a filling station attendant. At first, we didn't even know where Pearl Harbor was, but once we learned, we realized how serious the situation was. My parents were very upset, especially since two of my older brothers were of draft age.

North Zulch, Texas, was where I was on the weekend of the Pearl Harbor attack in December 1941. I had gone there from Houston to visit family with my brother. It was a quiet place, and we were unaware of the attack until we left for Houston and stopped in Conroe. North Zulch was important to me as my family home base during that time.

I was working in Houston, Texas, at the Port City Stockyards around the time of Pearl Harbor. I was chasing and selling cattle there, which I really enjoyed. Houston was my place of work, but I had gone to North Zulch for the weekend when the attack happened. After Pearl Harbor, I stayed in Houston and talked to my parents from there.

Conroe, Texas, was where I learned about the Pearl Harbor attack. My brother and I were returning there from North Zulch on Sunday afternoon when a filling station attendant told us the news. It was around 4 PM, and we were shocked and confused at first. Conroe was a key stop for us during that weekend.

I attended Texas A&M University and completed four years of ROTC training there, graduating the spring before Pearl Harbor. Although I had finished all my training, I wasn't commissioned until the following spring because I was not yet 21 years old on December 7, 1941. Texas A&M was where I prepared for my military career, which became very relevant after the war started.

After receiving my commission, I was ordered to report to Fort Riley, Kansas, where I stayed for about six months. I traveled there by the Rock Island Railroad. Fort Riley was my first Army base after Texas A&M, and I underwent further training there before moving on to Fort Bliss.

Kansas was where Fort Riley is located, and that's where I was stationed for about six months after getting my commission. It was my first Army posting, and I traveled there by train. Kansas marked the beginning of my active military service during World War II.

After Fort Riley, I was sent to Fort Bliss in El Paso, Texas. At Fort Bliss, I was assigned as a second lieutenant in Troop G of the 7th Cavalry Regiment. This was part of my preparation for combat duty during the war.

El Paso, Texas, is where Fort Bliss is located, and that's where I was stationed after Fort Riley. It was there that I served as a second lieutenant in the 7th Cavalry Regiment. El Paso was an important step in my military career before deployment overseas.

I was a second lieutenant in Troop G of the 7th Cavalry Regiment during my time at Fort Bliss and beyond. My ROTC training prepared me for this combat unit role. The 7th Cavalry was my first real combat assignment in the Army during World War II.

Before heading overseas, I was at Camp Stoneman, California, for about three weeks. This was a staging area before we embarked for Australia. Camp Stoneman was where we prepared to ship out as part of a large troop movement.

I arrived in Australia in July 1942 after an eight-day trip on the USS Monterey. We trained in Brisbane for about three weeks and then conducted maneuvers in the Outback on large cattle and sheep stations. The Australians were very friendly and reminded me a lot of Texans. I also encountered many New Zealanders training there, as most Australian men of draft age were already fighting in other theaters.

I traveled to Australia aboard the USS Monterey, which was a Matson liner converted to a troopship. There were about 6,000 of us on board, and the trip took about eight days, which was record time. The ship had peacetime crew and excellent food, and officers had separate quarters with two men per room. It was my first time on a ship, and many men got seasick during the voyage.

Brisbane, Australia, was where we arrived in July 1942 and where we trained for about three weeks. The city was very picturesque with several top-notch parks featuring native Australian animals like kangaroos and wallabies. We stayed in Brisbane for about six months before moving on to New Guinea. The Australians there were very welcoming, and the city left a strong impression on me.

While in Australia, I encountered quite a few New Zealanders who were training alongside us. Many of them were there because most Australian men of draft age had already been sent to fight in Europe and North Africa. Although I did not visit New Zealand itself, their presence was notable during our time in Brisbane.

I had applied for a two-week vacation to Sydney while stationed in Brisbane because I had heard a lot about the city and wanted to visit. However, the day I was supposed to leave for Sydney, we were loaded onto a ship to head to New Guinea instead. So unfortunately, I never got that chance to see Sydney during the war.

I was deployed to New Guinea after leaving Brisbane, traveling on a Liberty ship with about 200 men. We traveled in convoy with four destroyers escorting us from Townsville to Oro Bay on the north coast of New Guinea. We landed by unloading onto lighters since there were no docks at Oro Bay, and we were quartered right on the coastline. Malaria was a big problem there; almost all of us contracted it and had to take Atabrine throughout our time overseas.

I traveled to New Guinea on a Liberty ship carrying about 200 men. It was a troopship, and we traveled in convoy escorted by four destroyers from Townsville to Oro Bay. The Liberty ship was crucial for moving troops in the Pacific theater during the war.

Townsville, Australia, was a stopover point for us on the way to New Guinea. We waited there over Christmas for a convoy to arrive from the U.S. before continuing our journey. From Townsville, we traveled with four destroyers escorting us to Oro Bay, New Guinea.

While I was in the Pacific theater, we operated near the Solomon Islands, but malaria was less of a problem there compared to New Guinea. The Solomon Islands were part of the broader campaign area, but my main combat experiences were in New Guinea and the Admiralty Islands.

We also saw action in the Philippines during the war. The monkeys there were a memorable part of camp life, as they would chatter all night and alert us to nearby Japanese forces. Sadly, one of my best friends, a captain, died of malaria in the Philippines despite treatment. The Philippines was a tough and dangerous place for us during the campaign.

We were sent to the Admiralty Islands to reinforce the 5th Cavalry Division, which was struggling against about 6,000 Japanese Imperial Marines. The campaign lasted about six weeks, and we landed there from Australian destroyers using landing crafts. The fighting was intense, and our role was crucial in pushing back the Japanese forces.

The Japanese Imperial Marines were the enemy force we faced in the Admiralty Islands. There were about 6,000 of them, and they put up a fierce fight against the 5th Cavalry Division. Our unit was sent in as reinforcements to help push them back during a very tough campaign.

The 5th Cavalry Division was the first to land in the Admiralty Islands and faced heavy Japanese resistance. They were in bad shape and nearly wiped out, so our unit was sent in to reinforce them. We landed from Australian destroyers and helped complete the campaign in about six weeks.

The 31st Infantry Division was the unit we relieved when we landed at Oro Bay, New Guinea. Our landing there was peaceful compared to later campaigns. They had established positions before we arrived, which helped us settle in quickly.

We used landing crafts, sometimes called PT boats or infantry landing craft, to go ashore during amphibious assaults like the Admiralty Islands campaign. These boats were launched from destroyers and were the quickest way to get troops onto the beaches. They were essential for our operations in the Pacific islands.
